name: 10 Things the Artemis II Astronauts Might See on the Far Side of the Moon.

scenes.text: Number 10.
Seas Look at the moon
tonight, you will see dark patches covering nearly a third of the visible
surface. These are the Maria, ancient plains of
basalt, where volcanic lava flooded enormous craters billions of years
ago. They are so prominent that early astronomers mistook them for
oceans. For centuries, we assume the other side looked roughly the
same. It does
not. In 1959, the Soviet probe Luna 3 sent back the first photographs of the lunar far
side, and the images were so confusing that scientists initially assumed the camera had
malfunctioned. The dark volcanic plains that dominate our view of the moon simply were not
there. Where the near side is 31 % Maria, the far side is 1%.
The hemisphere that faces away from us is almost entirely pale, battered highland crust,
pockmarked by impacts that were never filled in by lava.
The current leading theory is that the Theia impact, the collision that created the moon in the first place,
left the near side crust thinner and more enriched with radioactive heat -producing elements like thorium and
uranium. The near side's interior stayed hotter for
longer. Magma found pathways to the
surface. The far side's crust was thicker, colder, more resistant. The lava never broke
through. When the Artemis astronauts look down at the far side of the moon, they will see a world that looks like it belongs to a different body
entirely. Two hemispheres of the same moon, separated by a geological chasm that has persisted for 4 .3
billion years. It is the first evidence that the moon is not what it appears to be. That was the surface. This next one is what is underneath it. 9.
Number 9.
Buried Mass In 2019, scientists analyzing gravity data from NASA's GRAIL spacecraft found something buried beneath the largest crater on the far side of
the moon. The gravity readings did not
add up. There was too much mass in the mantle. Far
too much. The anomaly weighs approximately 2
.18 quintillion kilograms. That is 2 .18,
followed by 18 zeros. It is at least five times larger than the
Big Island of Hawaii, and it sits more than 300 kilometers below the surface of
the South Pole Aitken Basin. Nobody is
entirely sure what it is. The most favored explanation is that it is the metallic core of the asteroid that excavated the
basin 4 .33 billion years ago. The impactor struck with such force
that it punched through the crust and embedded itself in the lunar mantle, where
it has remained, undisturbed, ever since. An alternative theory suggests it is a concentration of dense oxides
from the moon's ancient magma ocean pooled
during the final stages
of solidification. Either answer is disturbing. If it is the impactor's core, then the moon has been carrying the corpse of its attacker inside its
body for over 4 billion years. If it is crystallized magma ocean residue, then the moon's interior froze in a configuration that
modern physics still struggles to model. The Artemis astronauts
will fly directly over
this anomaly. They will feel nothing. But beneath them, something massive, something
ancient, something we cannot fully explain, is pulling on the fabric of space just a little bit harder than it should. Everything in the last entry is connected to what comes next.
Number 8.

scenes.text: Number 7.
Two -Faced Crust The moon's crust
is not uniform. On the near side, it about 60
kilometers thick. On the far side, it balloons to 80 kilometers
or more. This asymmetry is
not subtle. It represents a fundamental structural difference between two halves of the
same world. For decades, this was considered a
mild curiosity. Then the implications
became clear. The thicker far side crust acted as
a lid. Magma generated deep in the lunar interior could breach the thinner near side
relatively easily, flooding basins and creating the dark volcanic Maria visible
from Earth. On the far side, the lava had nowhere
to go. It beneath the surface, never reaching the
top. The volcanism that reshaped one hemisphere was physically blocked from reshaping the
other. A 2022 study published in Science Advances proposed that the South Pole -Atkin impact itself may have triggered this
entire asymmetry. The energy from the collision drove hemisphere
-scale mantle convection that concentrated heat -producing elements, thorium, titanium, and rare earth minerals
on the near side. The impact did not
just create a crater. It reorganized
the moon's internal One collision, 4 .3 billion ago, determined which half of the moon would be geologically alive, and which would be
geologically dead. The Artemis astronauts will cross from one hemisphere to
the other, from a world that erupted and flowed and resurfaced itself for billions of years, into world that could. That was structure. This next is the ghost.
Number 6.
The magnetic swirls The moon has no
global magnetic field. It lost its dynamo of
years ago, and yet, scattered across the far side are patches of crustal magnetism that should not
exist. They are concentrated, intense, and inexplicable under any simple model of lunar
evolution. Associated with these magnetic anomalies are features called lunar
swirls. They are flat. They cast no
shadows. They are bright, sinuous patterns painted the regolith
that look, from orbit, like someone dragged a cosmic brush across the surface.
The most is a swirl called Reiner which the
Artemis crew observed during their approach to the lunar far side. But the far
side hosts its own swirls, at Mare Ingini and Mare Marginis, and they are still. NASA describes these as
the moon's tan Pockets of residual magnetism as a
miniature shield, deflecting solar wind particles and preventing the surface beneath from darkening
time. The bright regions are protected. The dark lanes
flanking them are bombarded. The is a pattern looks but entirely natural,
or at least, that is the assumption. What created these magnetic is
still debated. Some are antipodal massive impacts the
opposite side of the moon, as the shock
wave from a collision traveled through the entire body and emerged as magnetism
on the other side. Others have no obvious source at all. Data from the Donnuri Orbiter revealed that magnetic fields
are more prevalent on the far side
than the near side, a finding that one researcher described as something that does not make sense. The Artemis astronauts are
flying over a world that lost its magnetic field eons ago but still
bears its fingerprints. Dead Things Should Not Leave marks this precise.
Number 5.

scenes.text: Number 3.
The far side of the moon is the quietest place accessible to human civilization,
not in the way a library is Quiet in
the way that a frequency spectrum zero anthropogenic contamination is quiet. Earth is a
screaming source of radio noise. Television broadcasts, cellular
networks, military radar, satellite communications, and the accumulated
electromagnetic output of billion people and their machines. This noise extends space.
It contaminates every radio telescope
on our planet and in orbit around it. For radio astronomers trying to
detect faint signals from the early universe, from the cosmic dark ages
before the first stars ignited, Earth's noise floor is a catastrophe. The far side of the
moon is shielded from all of it. The 3 ,474 kilometer diameter
of the moon itself acts as a physical barrier, blocking radio frequency that
Earth produces. A telescope placed on the
far side could detect signals in the 1 to 10 megahertz range, frequencies that are completely inaccessible
from Earth or even from Earth orbit our ionosphere absorbs them. These frequencies carry information the cosmic dark
ages, the period between 380 ,000 years after the
big bang and the formation of the first stars, roughly 200 million years later. No has ever observed this era. The
information exists. The signal there. We simply
cannot it from where we stand. The
far side of the moon is the only nearby
location in the system where we could. The Artemis II crew experienced this firsthand. For 40 minutes during
flyby, as the Orion spacecraft passed behind the
moon, all communication with Earth severed. NASA's space could not reach For those 40 minutes, four human beings were more
isolated from civilization than any humans in history,
drifting behind a that blocks the entire electromagnetic signature of their species. That was the silence. This
next one is what the silence might be hiding.
Number 2.
Flashes During the Artemis 2 flyby on April 6, 2026, the crew witnessed something that has never been observed by human eyes at this
proximity. As the moon passed between the Orion spacecraft and the sun, creating a solar eclipse visible only from behind the
moon, the astronauts watched the darkened lunar surface for transient flashes of
light. They reported
six, six flashes of light on an unlit lunar
surface, each one the death of a small meteoroid traveling at thousands of miles per hour, vaporizing on contact with the
regolith. Ground -based observers have recorded these
flashes before. Amateur astronomers occasionally catch them during
lunar eclipses. But the Artemis crew saw them from 4067 miles above
the surface. The closest any human has ever been to a live meteoroid impact on
another world. The moon has
no atmosphere. There is no friction to slow incoming debris, no shield to burn
it up. Every rock, every grain, every fragment of ancient asteroid or spent comet that crosses moon's path hits the at full orbital velocity.
The impact speeds range from 20 to 72 kilometers per second.
At those velocities, a particle size of a walnut releases energy equivalent to hand grenade. The flash
lasts a fraction of a second, then silence returns. These impacts
are rare, they happen continuously. Every day, the
moon struck by objects ranging from microscopic dust to boulders. The surface is not
static. It is being actively
sandblasted, reshaped in real time by a that has been for 4 .5 billion and shows no sign of stopping. The Artemis astronauts watch the world
get hit. Six times. In a few hours. They are looking at a process
that built the far side's battered face one impact at a time. And that process is running. Everything
you have heard so far describes what we know, this last one is about what we not.
Number 1.

aligned_segments.title: The missing seas
aligned_segments.body:
Number ten. The missing seas.
Look at the Moon tonight. You will see dark patches covering nearly a third of the visible surface. These are the maria, ancient plains of basalt where volcanic lava flooded enormous craters billions of years ago. They are so prominent that early astronomers mistook them for oceans. For centuries, we assumed the other side looked roughly the same.
It does not. In 1959, the Soviet probe Luna 3 sent back the first photographs of the lunar far side, and the images were so confusing that scientists initially assumed the camera had malfunctioned. The dark volcanic plains that dominate our view of the Moon simply were not there. Where the near side is 31 percent maria, the far side is 1 percent. The hemisphere that faces away from us is almost entirely pale, battered highland crust, pockmarked by impacts that were never filled in by lava.
The current leading theory is that the Theia impact, the collision that created the Moon in the first place, left the near side crust thinner and more enriched with radioactive, heat-producing elements like thorium and uranium. The near side's interior stayed hotter for longer. Magma found pathways to the surface. The far side's crust was thicker, colder, more resistant. The lava never broke through. When the Artemis astronauts look down at the far side of the Moon, they will see a world that looks like it belongs to a different body entirely. Two hemispheres of the same Moon, separated by a geological chasm that has persisted for 4.3 billion years.
It is the first evidence that the Moon is not what it appears to be.
That was the surface. This next one is what is underneath it.

aligned_segments.title: The buried mass
aligned_segments.body:
Number nine. The buried mass.
In 2019, scientists analyzing gravity data from NASA's GRAIL spacecraft found something buried beneath the largest crater on the far side of the Moon. The gravity readings did not add up. There was too much mass in the mantle. Far too much. The anomaly weighs approximately 2.18 quintillion kilograms. That is 2.18 followed by 18 zeros. It is at least five times larger than the Big Island of Hawaii, and it sits more than 300 kilometers below the surface of the South Pole-Aitken basin.
Nobody is entirely sure what it is. The most favored explanation is that it is the metallic core of the asteroid that excavated the basin 4.33 billion years ago. The impactor struck with such force that it punched through the crust and embedded itself in the lunar mantle, where it has remained, undisturbed, ever since. An alternative theory suggests it is a concentration of dense oxides from the Moon's ancient magma ocean, pooled during the final stages of solidification.
Either answer is disturbing. If it is the impactor's core, then the Moon has been carrying the corpse of its attacker inside its body for over four billion years. If it is crystallized magma ocean residue, then the Moon's interior froze in a configuration that modern physics still struggles to model. The Artemis astronauts will fly directly over this anomaly. They will feel nothing. But beneath them, something massive, something ancient, something we cannot fully explain, is pulling on the fabric of space just a little bit harder than it should.
Everything in the last entry is connected to what comes next.

aligned_segments.title: The deepest scar in the solar system
aligned_segments.body:
Number eight. The deepest scar in the solar system.
The South Pole-Aitken basin is not just the largest crater on the Moon. It is the largest confirmed impact structure in the entire solar system. It stretches 2,500 kilometers from rim to rim, roughly the distance from London to Moscow. It plunges nearly 9 kilometers below the surrounding terrain at its deepest point. The mountains ringing its edge reach elevations of 8,500 meters, just 350 meters shorter than Mount Everest.
The impact that created this basin occurred approximately 4.33 billion years ago. To put that in perspective, the oldest confirmed evidence of life on Earth dates to around 3.7 billion years ago. This crater predates every living thing that has ever existed on our planet by over 600 million years. The collision was so violent that it should have excavated material from 200 kilometers beneath the lunar surface, exposing the Moon's deep mantle. And yet, when scientists mapped the composition of the basin floor, they found something unexpected. The mantle material was not there. Instead, roughly 10 kilometers of crustal rock appears to line the bottom.
One explanation is that the impactor did not strike head-on. Computer simulations suggest a low-velocity projectile, perhaps 200 kilometers in diameter, struck at an angle of 30 degrees or less. It scraped rather than punctured. It carved the largest wound on any world we have ever studied, and somehow, it did not dig deep enough. The Artemis astronauts will see a portion of this basin's rim from their flyby trajectory. They will be looking at a scar so large it redefined our understanding of what violence means at a planetary scale.

aligned_segments.title: The two-faced crust
aligned_segments.body:
Number seven. The two-faced crust.
The Moon's crust is not uniform. On the near side, it averages about 60 kilometers thick. On the far side, it balloons to 80 kilometers or more. This asymmetry is not subtle. It represents a fundamental structural difference between two halves of the same world. For decades, this was considered a mild curiosity. Then the implications became clear.
The thicker far side crust acted as a lid. Magma generated deep in the lunar interior could breach the thinner near side relatively easily, flooding basins and creating the dark volcanic maria visible from Earth. On the far side, the lava had nowhere to go. It pooled beneath the surface, never reaching the top. The volcanism that reshaped one hemisphere was physically blocked from reshaping the other.
A 2022 study published in Science Advances proposed that the South Pole-Aitken impact itself may have triggered this entire asymmetry. The energy from the collision drove hemisphere-scale mantle convection that concentrated heat-producing elements, thorium, titanium, and rare earth minerals, on the near side. The impact did not just create a crater. It reorganized the Moon's internal chemistry. One collision, 4.33 billion years ago, determined which half of the Moon would be geologically alive and which half would be geologically dead.
The Artemis astronauts will cross from one hemisphere to the other. From a world that erupted and flowed and resurfaced itself for billions of years, into a world that never could.
That was the structure. This next one is the ghost.

aligned_segments.title: The magnetic swirls
aligned_segments.body:
Number six. The magnetic swirls.
The Moon has no global magnetic field. It lost its dynamo billions of years ago. And yet, scattered across the far side are localized patches of crustal magnetism that should not exist. They are concentrated, intense, and inexplicable under any simple model of lunar evolution.
Associated with these magnetic anomalies are features called lunar swirls. They are flat. They cast no shadows. They are bright, sinuous patterns painted onto the regolith that look, from orbit, like someone dragged a cosmic brush across the surface. The most famous is a swirl called Reiner Gamma, which the Artemis II crew observed during their approach to the lunar far side. But the far side hosts its own swirls, at Mare Ingenii and Mare Marginis, and they are stranger still.
NASA describes these swirls as the Moon's tan lines. Pockets of residual magnetism act as a miniature shield, deflecting solar wind particles and preventing the surface beneath from darkening over time. The bright regions are protected. The dark lanes flanking them are bombarded. The result is a pattern that looks deliberate but is entirely natural. Or at least, that is the current assumption.
What created these magnetic pockets is still debated. Some are antipodal to massive impacts on the opposite side of the Moon, as if the shockwave from a collision traveled through the entire body and emerged as magnetism on the other side. Others have no obvious source at all. Data from the Korean Danuri orbiter revealed that magnetic fields are more prevalent on the far side than the near side, a finding that one researcher described as something that does not make sense.
The Artemis astronauts are flying over a world that lost its magnetic field eons ago but still bears its fingerprints. Dead things should not leave marks this precise.

aligned_segments.title: The coldest place in the solar system
aligned_segments.body:
Number five. The coldest place in the solar system.
At the bottom of certain craters near the Moon's south pole, sunlight has not reached the surface in over two billion years. These are the permanently shadowed regions, places where the geometry of crater walls and the Moon's tiny axial tilt of 1.5 degrees conspire to create eternal night.
The temperatures inside these shadows have been measured at 25 Kelvin. That is minus 248 degrees Celsius. For reference, the surface of Pluto averages around 44 Kelvin. The coldest recorded temperature on the far side of the Moon is colder than the surface of the most distant dwarf planet in our solar system. It is, in fact, among the coldest naturally occurring temperatures ever measured on any body in the solar system.
These frozen vaults are not just cold. They are traps. Volatile compounds, water ice, carbon dioxide, ammonia, and methane, that wander across the lunar surface through a process called ballistic transport will occasionally land inside one of these shadows. Once inside, the molecules never leave. The temperature is too low for them to gain enough energy to escape. Over billions of years, these craters have been accumulating volatiles like a savings account that never allows withdrawals.
India's Chandrayaan-1 mission confirmed the presence of water ice in these regions. NASA's Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter and its ShadowCam instrument have since photographed the interior of these craters in unprecedented detail, using reflected earthshine to image terrain that has never seen direct sunlight. The Artemis astronauts will pass over these shadow zones. They will look into craters where the temperature sits 25 degrees above absolute zero, where water from comets that struck the Moon three billion years ago is still frozen exactly where it landed.
Time does not move in those craters. It accumulates.
That one was cold. This next one is old.

aligned_segments.title: The ancient lava plains that should not exist
aligned_segments.body:
Number four. The ancient lava plains that should not exist.
The far side of the Moon is not entirely devoid of volcanic deposits. A handful of small maria do exist, and they are concentrated almost exclusively inside the South Pole-Aitken basin. Mare Ingenii. The Apollo basin. Pockets of dark basalt in a sea of pale highland terrain.
But beneath the visible surface, scientists have discovered something more unsettling. Using spectroscopic data from India's Chandrayaan-1 orbiter and Japan's Kaguya spacecraft, researchers identified extensive deposits of cryptomaria. These are ancient lava flows that erupted billions of years ago and were subsequently buried under lighter-colored highland debris kicked up by later impacts. These ghost seas are invisible to the naked eye but detectable through the mineral signatures they leave behind when small craters punch through the overlying material and expose the dark basalt beneath.
The cryptomaria inside the South Pole-Aitken basin date to approximately 3.88 billion years ago, the Early Imbrian period. This means that despite the far side's thicker crust, despite all the geological barriers that should have prevented it, magma still managed to reach the surface in this one shattered region. The South Pole-Aitken impact thinned the crust enough to allow a brief volcanic episode. Then the impacts resumed, burying the evidence under meters of regolith.
The Artemis astronauts will fly over terrain that looks uniformly ancient and dead. Beneath it lies proof that the far side was not always silent. It erupted once. Then something buried the evidence, and it never erupted again.

aligned_segments.title: The radio silence zone
aligned_segments.body:
Number three.
The radio silence zone.
The far side of the Moon is the quietest place accessible to human civilization. Not quiet in the way a library is quiet. Quiet in the way that a frequency spectrum with zero anthropogenic contamination is quiet.
Earth is a screaming source of radio noise. Television broadcasts, cellular networks, military radar, satellite communications, and the accumulated electromagnetic output of eight billion people and their machines. This noise extends into space. It contaminates every radio telescope on our planet and in orbit around it. For radio astronomers trying to detect faint signals from the early universe, from the cosmic dark ages before the first stars ignited, Earth's noise floor is a catastrophe.
The far side of the Moon is shielded from all of it. The 3,474 kilometer diameter of the Moon itself acts as a physical barrier, blocking every radio frequency that Earth produces. A telescope placed on the far side could detect signals in the 1 to 10 megahertz range, frequencies that are completely inaccessible from Earth or even from Earth orbit because our planet's ionosphere absorbs them.
These frequencies carry information from the cosmic dark ages, the period between 380,000 years after the Big Bang and the formation of the first stars, roughly 200 million years later. No telescope has ever observed this era. The information exists. The signal is there. We simply cannot hear it from where we stand. The far side of the Moon is the only nearby location in the solar system where we could.
The Artemis II crew experienced this silence firsthand. For 40 minutes during their flyby, as the Orion spacecraft passed behind the Moon, all communication with Earth was severed. NASA's Deep Space Network could not reach them. For those 40 minutes, four human beings were more isolated from civilization than any humans in history, drifting behind a world that blocks the entire electromagnetic signature of their species.
That was the silence. This next one is what the silence might be hiding.

aligned_segments.title: The meteoroid flashes
aligned_segments.body:
Number two. The meteoroid flashes.
During the Artemis II flyby on April 6, 2026, the crew witnessed something that has never been observed by human eyes at this proximity. As the Moon passed between the Orion spacecraft and the Sun, creating a solar eclipse visible only from behind the Moon, the astronauts watched the darkened lunar surface for transient flashes of light.
They reported six. Six flashes of light on an unlit lunar surface, each one the death of a small meteoroid traveling at thousands of miles per hour, vaporizing on contact with the regolith. Ground-based observers have recorded these flashes before. Amateur astronomers occasionally catch them during lunar eclipses. But the Artemis crew saw them from 4,067 miles above the surface, the closest any human has ever been to a live meteoroid impact on another world.
The Moon has no atmosphere. There is no friction to slow incoming debris. No shield to burn it up. Every rock, every grain, every fragment of ancient asteroid or spent comet that crosses the Moon's path hits the surface at full orbital velocity. The impact speeds range from 20 to 72 kilometers per second. At those velocities, a particle the size of a walnut releases energy equivalent to a hand grenade. The flash lasts a fraction of a second. Then silence returns.
These impacts are not rare. They happen continuously. Every day, the Moon is struck by objects ranging from microscopic dust to boulders. The surface is not static. It is being actively sandblasted, reshaped in real time by a bombardment that has been ongoing for 4.5 billion years and shows no sign of stopping.
The Artemis astronauts watched a world get hit. Six times. In a few hours. They are looking at a process that built the far side's battered face one impact at a time, and that process is still running.
Everything you have heard so far describes what we know. This last one is about what we do not.

aligned_segments.title: The terrain no human has ever seen
aligned_segments.body:
Number one. The terrain no human has ever seen.
On April 6, 2026, the Artemis II crew became the first humans in history to observe portions of the lunar far side that had never been seen by human eyes. The Apollo astronauts orbited the Moon at roughly 60 miles altitude and observed large sections of the far side. But Artemis II flew a different trajectory, a free-return path that carried the Orion spacecraft 4,067 miles from the surface, at angles and lighting conditions that no previous crew had experienced.
At their farthest point, the crew was 252,756 miles from Earth. Only 20 percent of the far side was illuminated by direct sunlight during the flyby. But the low sun angle created something that orbital photographs cannot replicate. Shadows stretched across the cratered surface, carving depth into ancient impact basins, revealing ridges and slopes and textural details that vanish under full illumination. The crew photographed ancient lava flows, crater chains, and surface cracks formed as the Moon slowly shrank over geological time. They described differences in color, brightness, and texture that provide clues about the composition and history of the lunar far side.
The crew also proposed names for two craters they could see with the naked eye. One they called Integrity, after their spacecraft. The other they named Carroll, after the late wife of mission commander Reid Wiseman. The proposals will be formally submitted to the International Astronomical Union.
But here is what matters. The far side of the Moon is 7.4 million square kilometers of terrain. That is larger than the continent of Australia. Every Apollo mission combined observed only a fraction of it, from fixed orbital paths, under fixed lighting. Artemis II saw it differently. Artemis III and the missions that follow will land on it. They will walk on it. They will drill into it.
And what they will find is a world that has been recording the history of the solar system for 4.5 billion years, with no atmosphere to erode the record, no plate tectonics to recycle the evidence, no oceans to dissolve the clues. Every crater is a timestamp. Every mineral grain is a data point. Every shadow hides a chapter we have not yet read.
We have been staring at one side of the Moon since the first human looked up. The other side has been watching the rest of the universe the entire time. And it remembers everything.
